title: The science of why music gives us chills
text:
You know the feeling. A chord resolves in an unexpected way, a singer hits a note you weren’t ready for, and suddenly the hairs on your arms stand up. Your skin prickles. Your chest tightens, pleasurably.
Scientists call it frisson (from the French for “shiver”). And understanding why it happens has revealed something profound about how the brain makes sense of music — and perhaps of beauty itself.

title: The science of why music gives us chills
text:
The prediction machine
The brain, it turns out, is essentially a prediction machine. At every moment, it is building models of what is about to happen in the world and constantly comparing those predictions to what actually occurs.
Music exploits this ruthlessly. A melody sets up expectations — patterns, rhythms, harmonic structures — and then plays with them. A chord that resolves differently than expected. A beat that lands a fraction late. A voice that soars higher than the melody seemed to be heading.
When those violations of expectation are pleasantly surprising — when the brain’s prediction was wrong, but gloriously so — the result can be frisson.

title: The science of why music gives us chills
section: Who gets it and who doesn’t
text:
Who gets it and who doesn’t
Not everyone experiences frisson. Research suggests roughly 55–65% of people report ever feeling it. A 2018 study from the University of Southern California found that people who frequently experience frisson have stronger connections between their auditory cortex and areas associated with emotional processing.
In other words, in some brains, the pathways between “hearing music” and “feeling emotions” are more richly connected. Those people don’t just hear music — they’re wired to feel it more viscerally.






Try it yourself



Pay attention to where the chills arrive in a piece of music you love. Research shows they most often occur at moments of harmonic surprise, dynamic contrast, or when a new voice or instrument enters unexpectedly.

title: The science of why music gives us chills
text:
The dopamine connection
Frisson is accompanied by a release of dopamine — the same neurotransmitter involved in pleasure, reward, and anticipation. Brain imaging studies show that the dopamine release begins before the musical climax — during the moment of anticipation — and peaks at the moment the payoff arrives.

“Music is perhaps the only stimulus that reliably activates the reward system in a way that doesn’t involve any survival need.” — Robert Zatorre, McGill University

The honest answer is that we don’t fully know why we evolved this response. But frisson is a window into that question — a moment when the machinery of the brain’s emotional response becomes briefly, thrillingly visible.
Put on a song that always gets you. Notice the anticipation. Notice where the chills arrive. You’re watching your prediction engine fire.

title: Workshops & Training
Science Writing for Beginners
Science Writing for Beginners
text:
What we’ll cover

How to find the human story inside a research paper
Structure: the inverted pyramid vs. narrative approaches
Writing the first sentence (the hardest part)
Plain language techniques that don’t dumb things down
How to pitch your story to a publication or blog

No writing experience necessary. Bring a piece of your own research or a paper you find interesting — we’ll workshop it together.






What to bring



A research paper or topic you’d like to write about, and a laptop or notebook. Tea and coffee provided.

title: Why ants are better engineers than us
text:
Deep in the rainforests of Central America, beneath mounds that can stretch six metres underground, leafcutter ant colonies are solving engineering problems that have stumped human architects for decades.
These colonies — some housing up to eight million individuals — maintain an almost constant internal temperature of 25°C, regardless of what the jungle outside is doing. They manage humidity to within a few percentage points. They circulate fresh air through a network of tunnels without a single fan or ventilation system. They’ve been doing this for 45 million years.

title: Why ants are better engineers than us
text:
The tunnel network
The secret lies in the shape and arrangement of their tunnels. Researchers at the University of Campinas discovered that leafcutter ants construct their tunnels at specific angles and diameters that exploit natural convection — warm air rises, cool air sinks, and the geometry of the nest does the rest.
When the fungus gardens at the colony’s centre produce metabolic heat, it rises through central shafts and escapes through dozens of exit holes near the mound’s surface. Meanwhile, cooler, fresh air is drawn in through lower entrances. The system requires no energy input from the ants themselves. It simply works.






Key finding



Leafcutter ant tunnels are oriented at precise angles that maximise passive airflow — a principle now being studied for application in low-energy building design.



“They’ve solved a problem that costs human buildings enormous amounts of energy to manage, and they did it without any conscious engineering decisions.” — Dr. Tomer Czaczkes, University of Regensburg

title: Why ants are better engineers than us
text:
What we’re learning
Engineers are paying attention. Biomimicry — the practice of drawing design inspiration from nature — has produced Velcro (from burdock burrs), wind turbine blades (from humpback whale fins), and building facades that self-clean (from the lotus leaf).
Ant nest architecture may be the next frontier. Several research groups are now studying whether ant-inspired ventilation principles could reduce the energy consumption of office buildings, data centres, and underground infrastructure.

title: Why ants are better engineers than us
section: The engineers who don’t know they’re engineering
text:
The engineers who don’t know they’re engineering
Perhaps the most remarkable thing about leafcutter ants is that no individual ant understands any of this. There is no architect, no foreman, no blueprint. Each ant follows simple local rules — deposit soil here, avoid tunnel diameters smaller than x — and the global result is sophisticated engineering.
This is called stigmergy: coordination through the environment itself. Ants leave chemical signals in the soil that influence where the next ant digs. Over millions of interactions, these simple rules produce complex, functional structures.
It’s a reminder that intelligence — or at least the appearance of it — doesn’t require a central controller. The colony thinks, even if no individual ant does.
For human engineers, that’s both humbling and inspiring.

title: Talking to the public about uncertain science
text:
Science is, by its nature, uncertain. Results are provisional. Findings need replication. Confidence intervals are ranges, not points. Every paper comes with caveats.
The public, and often the media, wants certainty. “Scientists discover…” “New research proves…” “Study shows…”
Bridging that gap — communicating genuine scientific uncertainty without either frightening people or being dismissed as wishy-washy — is one of the hardest skills in science communication.

title: Talking to the public about uncertain science
section: The instinct to oversimplify is understandable
text:
The instinct to oversimplify is understandable
When you’ve spent three years on a study that found a modest correlation between gut bacteria and anxiety in a sample of 200 people, you want people to care. The temptation to say “scientists discover gut bacteria causes anxiety” is real, even if you know that’s not what your data shows.
The problem isn’t dishonesty. It’s the gap between how scientists talk among themselves (hedged, conditional, probabilistic) and how everyone else talks (concrete, causal, decisive).

title: Talking to the public about uncertain science
section: Uncertainty isn’t the same as ignorance
text:
Uncertainty isn’t the same as ignorance
The first thing I tell scientists preparing to speak publicly is to distinguish between two kinds of uncertainty:

We don’t know — honest about gaps in the evidence
We know it’s complicated — actually informative, showing science is doing the hard work

Framing uncertainty as complexity rather than ignorance shifts the tone from “science is unreliable” to “science is doing the hard work of understanding a complicated world.”






The golden rule



Never bury caveats in the last paragraph. Audiences who discover them later feel misled — and trust, once broken, is very hard to rebuild.

title: Talking to the public about uncertain science
text:
The honest middle
The honest middle sounds something like this:

“We found X. This is a first step — the finding needs to be replicated and we don’t yet know the mechanism. But it points in an interesting direction that’s worth pursuing.”

That’s it. State what you found, contextualise how preliminary it is, and say why it matters enough to share.
Most audiences can handle nuance. What they can’t handle — and what erodes trust in science — is finding out later that they were told a cleaner version of the truth than the truth deserved.
